manualExchange.title: Manual Copy-Paste
manualExchange.content: We don’t “send” the key over the network. You copy and paste it out-of-band. That’s secure as long as the channel you use to share the key (e.g., meeting in person) is trusted. No server is involved.

keyExchangeProblem.title: The Key Exchange Problem
keyExchangeProblem.content: If a server sends 'Bob's public key', an attacker could replace it with their own (man-in-the-middle) and read everything. Distribution and verification are the two halves of the problem.

outOfBand.title: Out-of-band Verification
outOfBand.content: Signal/WhatsApp: each device has a fingerprint; you scan the other's QR code or compare safety numbers. That’s out-of-band verification of the key the server already gave you.

keyDirectory.title: Key Directory / Server
keyDirectory.content: Apps like Signal fetch keys from a server using protocols (like X3DH) so the server never sees the shared secret. They offer manual verification to protect against compromised servers.

pki.title: PKI & Certificate Authorities
pki.content: For websites, the 'key' is the server's certificate. The browser checks its signature against trusted CAs. No manual paste; the key is exchanged in the TLS handshake.

tofu.title: Trust On First Use (TOFU)
tofu.content: Used by SSH: First time you connect, you accept the server's key. Later, if the key changes, you get a warning (possible MITM).

pgp.title: PGP Web of Trust
pgp.content: Public keys are uploaded to keyservers. Others sign Alice's key; if you trust the signers, you trust the key. Or you do a fingerprint check out-of-band.

secureContext.title: Secure Context
secureContext.content: window.isSecureContext is true only on HTTPS or localhost. Web Crypto and WebAuthn require it to limit exposure to MITM.

digest.title: Digest (Hashing)
digest.content: crypto.subtle.digest() provides SHA-256 for integrity and fingerprints. Same input → same output; one-way. Not for password storage.

signVerify.title: Sign & Verify
signVerify.content: RSA-PSS or ECDSA prove authenticity: only the holder of the private key can produce a valid signature; anyone with the public key can verify.

spkiFormat.title: SPKI Format
spkiFormat.content: Subject Public Key Info (SPKI) is a standard format for public keys. In this demo we use base64 encoding.

rsaOaep.title: RSA-OAEP Limitations
rsaOaep.content: RSA-OAEP (2048-bit) can only encrypt very short messages (~190 bytes). For longer messages, apps use hybrid encryption (AES + RSA).
